summary: Hotz presents his framework for building artificial general intelligence based on MuZero's architecture: representation, dynamics, and prediction functions. He argues that machine learning provides better intuition for understanding the brain than neuroscience, and that we'll truly understand life only when we can build it. He details comma.ai's specific implementation using EfficientNet for vision, GRU for temporal modeling, and explains why pure reinforcement learning doesn't work for real-world driving due to incomplete simulators and missing reward functions. The post concludes that driving is the ideal applied AI problem because solving it correctly creates reusable components for harder challenges like cleaning and eventually artificial life.
key_points: Three fundamental functions define intelligence: representation (sensor processing), dynamics (temporal modeling), and prediction (action selection)
Understanding comes from building: 'We will know we understand a brain once we can build a brain'
Machine learning provides better intuition about cognition than neuroscience or cognitive psychology
Real-world RL is limited by incomplete simulators (agents can't respond to counterfactuals) and missing reward functions
Driving is the optimal AI problem: simpler than cleaning but with reusable solutions, expert data availability, and clear reward signals
money_quotes: We will know we understand life once we can build life. We will know we understand a brain once we can build a brain.
I have a far better intuition for how the brain works from studying machine learning than I ever got from neuroscience or cognitive psychology.
There's no such thing as consciousness, we are all p-zombies, and philosophy is for high schoolers who just discovered smoking weed.
Nobody would be happy if their car 'explored', it should only 'exploit'.
If you can't solve driving, you certainly can't solve cleaning. And if you can't solve cleaning, you certainly can't solve life.
And you'll forget about that Chinese room so fast, when this 'machine life' is cooking and cleaning and driving you around and making you fall in love with it.
There's nothing about traffic cones in your DNA, so if you are trying to build a driving agent, I don't think hiring a guy to write cone handling software is very smart.
tone: technical, provocative, dismissive of philosophy, confident
key_insight: The path to artificial general intelligence runs through solving driving correctly, because the architecture that works for autonomous vehicles—learned representation, dynamics, and prediction—is the same architecture that will eventually produce artificial life.
